title: Wet-felted small rug
subtitle: A 60 x 40 cm bathroom or bedside rug in chunky Corriedale, fulled to a dense firm cloth
excerpt: A wet-felted rug uses the same technique as a flat panel but with a much larger roving quantity and significantly more fulling time. This guide covers a 60 x 40 cm rug in natural Corriedale, which is more cost-effective than merino at this scale and felts to a dense, durable cloth. Expect two to three hours of work and 40-45% take-up from the pre-felt lay.
sourceNotes: Hand-felted domestic rugs are a traditional Central Asian and Scandinavian form. The domestic-scale wet-felted rug method described here is standard practice as taught in contemporary guild workshops.

content.text: rather than merino: it is a third of the price at the weight needed and produces a harder-wearing cloth more suited to floor use. The pre-felt lay for a 60 x 40 cm finished rug is approximately 100 x 67 cm, allowing for 40% take-up.
is necessary to achieve the density that makes a rug durable enough for foot traffic.

items.name: Hot water and cold water access (bath or large sink)
Bamboo rolling mat, large (120 cm or wider)
Felting net or old net curtain
Non-slip rubber mat or carpet gripper strip (for backing, optional)

content.text: Mark out a 100 x 67 cm rectangle on the bamboo mat with tape or chalk. Lay four cross-hatched layers of Corriedale roving within the rectangle, keeping the edges straight. Each layer should be dense: when you hold a corner to the light, individual fibres should not be visible. At this scale, laying takes 20-30 minutes.

Lay the felting net over the entire panel. Pour hot soapy water over the net in sections until the whole panel is saturated. Press with flat palms to expel air. Begin rubbing through the net in straight passes from top to bottom, then side to side. After 10 minutes, test a corner: if the surface holds without the net, remove it and continue rubbing directly. This stage takes 20-30 minutes.

Roll the panel tightly in the bamboo mat and roll 100 passes under your body weight. Unroll, rotate 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat until the rug has reduced to approximately 80 x 53 cm (about two-thirds of the way to finished size). At this point, transition to throwing: take the rug in both hands, roll it loosely, and throw it against the mat surface 30-40 times. This vigorous fulling drives the remaining shrinkage efficiently.

When the rug measures approximately 62 x 42 cm and the surface resists a hard scratch, rinse in warm water and then plunge briefly into cold. Press out excess water by rolling in old towels. Lay flat on a clean surface and stretch gently to the target dimensions. Pin the edges to a foam board or leave on the floor to dry flat. Drying takes 24-48 hours depending on temperature and airflow.

Hand-wash in cool water with a small amount of wool wash. Do not machine-wash: the agitation cycle will full the rug further and change its dimensions. Press water out in a towel and dry flat. A felt rug can be spot-cleaned with a damp cloth in situ.

items.fix: Roll equally from both ends and rotate 90 degrees after every 30 passes.
items.cause: Rolling was always from the same starting end
items.problem: The rug shrinks unevenly and one end is smaller than the other

items.fix: Re-wet the rug with hot soapy water and full more vigorously. A properly fulled Corriedale rug will not pill under normal foot traffic.
items.cause: Insufficient fulling: the cloth was not dense enough at completion
items.problem: The rug surface is pilling after a few weeks of use

items.fix: When laying, concentrate extra roving at the edges. During rolling, push the mat edges inward with your hands to include them in the rolling pressure.
items.cause: Roving lay was thinner at the edges, or rolling pressure was not applied at the edges
items.problem: The edges are thinner and more fragile than the centre
